items.en: I don't need to know. Their contact is their testimony. Mine is mine. God meets each of us where we are. I'm not the gatekeeper of who's allowed to make contact. I'm just one witness among many.

fixtures.en: This sentence does not have a Japanese translation — switching languages will show this exact English text, or the raw key, depending on whether the missing-key fallback has shipped yet.

items.title: Old widget filenames kept for backward compat
items.description: widgets/lang-fab.js and widgets/demo-fab.js are dead copies kept because existing CDN pages reference them. New canonical names are fab-lang.js and fab-demo.js. Accumulates until pages are ported.

items.title: No HTML dead link scanner in CI
items.description: Renaming a slug requires manually updating all internal hrefs across pages. No automated check catches a missed touch point before merge.

items.title: No atomic slug rename script
items.description: Renaming a slug touches 7 layers: nodes.json, index.json, HTML filename, string scope keys, compiled bundles, God Script filename, and internal links. No tooling does this atomically — each rename is a manual multi-step operation.

items.title: specimen-architectural-wisdoms has no string bundle
items.description: Page renders static HTML. No compiled scope bundle exists for pages.specimen-architectural-wisdoms. Localization is a no-op for this page until a source file is written and strings-compile is run.

items.title: i18n string bundling strategy does not scale to multiple languages
items.description: JA strings are currently fetched and cached per page scope-combination (e.g. vex-lang-strings-1.0.0-ja-pages.specimen-full-translation,specimens). This is fine at 2 languages and 88 nodes. Adding a third language (Korean, Mandarin, etc.) multiplies the fetch surface and localStorage footprint linearly per language. At global scale — many languages × many nodes × many users switching language on their first visit to each new page — the scope-keyed approach compounds into exponential CDN cost and localStorage pressure. The natural migration is arc-chunked language bundles: one file per arc per language, SW-preloaded on arc entry. This is a build-script change (build-vextreme.js + fab-lang.js + build-sw.js), not a content change — slugs, arcs, and string source files do not move. Decision must be made before adding a third language. Do not design the third language's pipeline on the current per-scope-combo model.
items.triggerCondition: Before adding any language beyond English and Japanese
items.migrationPath: Arc-chunked bundles: one strings.{arc}.{lang}.json per arc, pre-fetched by SW on arc entry. Build script assembles chunks; browser receives without assembling.

items.title: Current architecture has no structured-concurrency model — a known ceiling once server logic or multi-threaded application scope enters the ecosystem
items.description: This repo is plain Node.js build scripts plus simple fetch/cache logic in the browser — single-threaded, no shared-memory parallelism, no genuine concurrent-thread danger anywhere in it today. That's fine for the current scope and is not a bug. It becomes one the moment the ecosystem grows to include actual server-side logic or applications with real concurrency needs (multiple simultaneous requests mutating shared state, background workers, task queues) — plain JS/Node gives no structural protection there beyond discipline (manually wiring AbortController at every async boundary, remembering to cancel stale work), the same 'good practice, not auto practice' gap named in docs/culture.md's AI-instance-reflection section. Reasoned through at length in a Session 021 conversation: JS is structurally safer than Kotlin/JVM against classic thread-level race conditions (no OS-thread-shared-memory parallelism exists in this codebase's execution model, so that bug class is currently impossible here, not just avoided) — but Kotlin's structured concurrency (coroutine scopes, automatic cancellation propagation) and its stricter null-safety type system are real, specific advantages once genuine concurrent/server-side logic is in scope, advantages TypeScript's strict mode only partially matches (TypeScript has real escape hatches — any, runtime type erasure — that Kotlin's type system doesn't).
items.context: No specific file yet — this is a language/runtime-architecture decision, not a code location. Relevant once lib/ grows server-side logic or this ecosystem gains an application layer with real concurrency.
items.triggerCondition: Before adding server-side logic or any application with genuine concurrent/multi-threaded requirements — do not discover this ceiling mid-build.
items.migrationPath: Two-tier decision, not proposed as one migration: (1) near-term, adopt TypeScript with strictNullChecks for the existing JS layer — same toolchain, no JVM dependency, closes most of the null-safety gap and forces compiler-enforced reference consistency in build-script-to-build-script calls (see the JSON-vs-typed-references reasoning in the same Session 021 conversation). (2) If and when server/application/mobile scope becomes real, seriously re-evaluate Kotlin (or Kotlin Multiplatform specifically if Android/iOS is also real) for that new scope — it would deliver both the concurrency-safety and platform-reach cases at once, whereas adopting it now for the current JS-only, single-threaded scope would add JVM toolchain cost without a concurrency problem yet to solve.

items.title: lib/check-lattice-edges.js — verify docs/lattice-map.json's reads/writes/loadedBy claims against actual code
items.description: docs/lattice-map.json's edges are hand-written and have never been checked against the source they describe. Session 022 found two stale claims by hand (a comment misdescribing data/arcs.json's key casing in lib/build-archives.js; two files' lattice context claiming build-status.js/build-ecosystem-hub.js were 'not yet in CI' after they'd been wired into .github/workflows/build-index.yml). Same failure shape lib/check-design-tokens.js already catches for CSS custom properties: a written claim silently drifting from what's actually true. Named as the correct next increment (not the bigger consolidation/deletion gesture in od-008) in docs/architecture/13-intent-driven-operations.md — any future attempt to trace connectors for a riskier operation depends on the map describing those connectors accurately first. Scope: for each lattice node, confirm its claimed reads/writes targets are actually referenced in that file's source, and flag any file that references a lattice-mapped node without appearing in that node's loadedBy list. Informational severity, wired into CI the same way as the other three drift detectors.
items.requires: none — same pattern as check-design-tokens.js/check-key-alignment.js, no new infrastructure

items.title: PWA icons
items.description: icons/icon-192.png and icons/icon-512.png are referenced in manifest.json but not committed. PWA installability prompt will not appear until both exist.

items.title: Port remaining blocked pages to God Scripts
items.description: 5 pages ported (Session 013). 3 remain blocked: claude-answers-the-doubt (needs arc-nav as a God Script feature — see pe-007), restoration-protocol (uses shell.js, needs investigation), specimen-architectural-wisdoms (needs compiled string bundle). Run node lib/audit-pages.js for current status.
items.blockedBy: arc-nav.js widget not yet implemented (pe-007 registry slot is ready) for claude-answers-the-doubt

items.title: Spiral portal FAB (vex-fab.js)
items.description: Unified radial-menu FAB replacing lang-fab + demo-fab. Deferred until page-porting phase is underway — no point shipping a new widget before the pages that will use it.

items.title: Missing-key fallback: show EN text instead of raw key string
items.description: When a JA translation key is absent, the browser shows the raw key string rather than falling back to the EN text. Affects the intentional gap in claude-answers-the-doubt layer.04.qa.04.answer.

items.title: strings-check: audit HTML for translatable elements missing data-i18n
items.description: strings-check.js validates existing data-i18n attributes but does not detect elements that should have data-i18n and don't. Silent coverage gap.

items.title: Slug rename tooling
items.description: A lib/rename-slug.js that atomically updates all 7 touch points of a slug rename: nodes.json, index.json, HTML filename, string scope keys, compiled bundles, God Script filename, and internal links across pages.

items.title: Unified feature registry — collapse CORE_MODULES and FEATURE_WIDGET into one table
items.description: Shipped in Session 013. CORE_MODULES + FEATURE_WIDGET replaced by a single FEATURES registry in lib/build-vextreme.js: { sw: { default: true }, lang: { default: false }, demo: { default: false }, arc-nav: { default: false }, spiral-fab: { default: false }, theme: { default: false } }. Feature.SW and Feature.ARC_NAV added to lib/vex-config.js. God Script output is byte-identical — purely an internal code structure change. arc-nav slot registered for future implementation.

items.title: Offline mode configuration FAB (fab-offline.js)
items.description: A new feature widget that surfaces SW cache state to users and gives them explicit control over what is pre-loaded for offline use. Shows: whether SW is active, which pages/arcs are available offline, a 'download this arc in Japanese' control that pre-fetches JA string bundles for the current arc on demand. Addresses td-006 (multi-language scaling) by replacing speculative pre-fetching with user-intentional caching — the user decides what to download, not the build script. Feature-flagged ('offline' feature) so it only appears on pages that declare it. Requires pe-007 (unified feature registry) to be included as a God Script module.

items.title: Collapse lib/build-archives.js onto data/index.json instead of re-deriving from nodes.json/arcs-v2.json independently
items.description: Session 022's auto-discovery feature (lib/auto-discover-nodes.js) had to be wired into both lib/build-index.js and lib/build-archives.js separately, because build-archives.js reads data/nodes.json and data/arcs-v2.json directly and re-derives its own slugMap-equivalent, rather than consuming data/index.json's already-computed slugMap/arcMap/departmentMap the way every other downstream consumer does (see docs/architecture.md: 'everything downstream reads index.json; nothing downstream reads nodes.json or arcs-v2.json directly' — build-archives.js is the one file that violates this today). The duplication was avoidable only because both call sites share the same discoverOrphanNodes() function; a future automation point would need the same two-place wiring again. Collapsing build-archives.js to read index.json directly would mean any future index.json-level enhancement (auto-discovery or otherwise) only ever needs to land in one place.

items.title: Department dashboard depth — dedicated transcript library, God-Script wiring granularity
items.description: Session 022 shipped the first checkpoint of the department axis (data/departments.json, department/workType fields on nodes, index.json departmentMap/departmentMeta, an ecosystem-hub.html Departments panel) deliberately scoped to structure + accessibility only, per Victor's direction to 'hone the placeholders' later. Two concrete follow-ups named explicitly at that time, not yet built: (1) wip/silent-god.json and its record-transcripts workType siblings are expected to eventually become their own 'library' with a dedicated dashboard, not just entries in the shared Departments panel; (2) departmentMap's per-node status is currently a single `ported` boolean (does pages/{slug}.html exist) — it does not yet distinguish 'ported' from 'wired to a God Script' the way lib/audit-pages.js does for the whole site. Extending departmentMap to reuse audit-pages.js's wiring logic (would require exporting its pure functions instead of running immediately) is the natural next step once there's more than one Media page to make the distinction meaningful.

items.title: Expand LATTICE coverage beyond the current 27 nodes (64% of lib/+widgets/)
items.description: docs/lattice-map.json covers 27 of ~33 eligible files under lib/ and widgets/ (64% coverage per lib/check-design-tokens.js-style accounting in data/status.json._meta.lattice). Session 019 added lib/logger.js, lib/logger-codes.js, lib/validate-blueprint.js, lib/build-sitemap.js, lib/strings-check.js, lib/build-index-page.js. lib/build-lattice-headers.js (Session 014) makes adding a node cheap — it generates the in-file header automatically, so expansion is JSON-authoring work only, not comment-formatting work. Remaining candidates: lib/strings-export.js, lib/strings-import.js, widgets/fab-demo.js, widgets/demo-fab.js, widgets/lang-fab.js (legacy pre-rename copies), lib/shell.js, lib/vextreme.js, lib/archive-renderer.js (v1 system). Grep for LATTICE:BEGIN vs total file count in lib/+widgets/ shows current coverage at any time.

items.claim: sw.js is served correctly from /Vextreme/sw.js on GitHub Pages and the service worker registers successfully
Scoped fetch + category CDN paths (scopes/{category}/pages.{slug}.{lang}.json) resolve correctly against the live CDN post-merge
archives.html GitHub Actions auto-rebuild fires correctly on push to main
index.html root nav renders correctly on vgong24.github.io/Vextreme (arc nav mounts, all arc dots appear)
vextreme-demo.html live index.json fetch resolves on GitHub Pages and renders the 88-node / 16-arc stats

items.title: Staged/proposal execution for higher-blast-radius content-management gestures (consolidation, deletion, connector rewiring)
items.description: config/content-intents.json + lib/apply-content-intents.js (Session 022) handle one gesture — place (department/workType/arc for one slug) — which is low-stakes: reversible by re-declaring the intent, and a wrong placement is cosmetic. Victor described a longer-term gesture in the same shape but much higher stakes: detect duplicate/redundant content or modules across departments, synthesize which files are actually equivalent, and consolidate — create a new shared file, delete the originals, rewire every connector that depended on them. A wrong caller-graph trace here breaks whatever depended on the deleted file, possibly silently, and 'verify the final state matches intent' for a deletion requires exercising every path that touched the old files, not just confirming the new file exists — categorically different from placement's verification, which just reads the arc/department back.
considerations: Do not design this now — a staging/proposal mechanism designed against zero real consolidation cases in this repo is very likely wrong in ways that only show up once a real case exists to design against, same reasoning already applied to od-003 and od-007 at different scales

The shape it should eventually have, not yet a spec: a 'propose' stage returning the full trace (what it found, what it would delete, what it would rewire) before anything destructive executes, human confirmation required between propose and apply, and verification that means confirming nothing dependent on the old state broke — not just that the new state exists
Blocked on pe-012 in practice, not just in principle: any connector trace this gesture would need to run depends on docs/lattice-map.json's edges actually being true, which nothing currently verifies
Promote to a planned enhancement once a real duplicate-content or module-consolidation case actually exists to design against, not before

items.title: Should the per-scope JA string bundling strategy change before a 3rd language is added?
items.description: JA strings are fetched and cached per page scope-combination (see widgets/fab-lang.js loadStringsForLang). This is fine at 2 languages and 88 nodes — each combination is small and cache-friendly. A 3rd language multiplies the fetch surface and localStorage footprint linearly per language, and at enough languages × nodes the scope-keyed approach compounds into real CDN cost and localStorage pressure. The candidate alternative is arc-chunked language bundles (one file per arc per language, SW-preloaded on arc entry) — a build-script change to lib/build-vextreme.js, widgets/fab-lang.js, and lib/build-sw.js, not a content change; slugs, arcs, and string source files would not move.
considerations: Do nothing until a 3rd language is actually requested — the current model works fine at 2 languages, and premature migration risks solving a problem that may never arrive in this exact shape

Migrate to arc-chunked bundles now, while only 2 languages exist — smaller migration surface than doing it after a 3rd language is already live and needs the old and new schemes to coexist during transition
Migrate only the highest-traffic arcs first, leaving low-traffic content on per-scope bundling — a hybrid that adds a second lookup path but limits the blast radius of the change

items.title: Should build-time content synthesis use a live LLM call, or stay session-authored?
items.description: The ecosystem hub's system-state content (architecture summary, cross-file narrative) could in principle be generated by calling a cheap/free LLM from lib/build-ecosystem-hub.js at build time, synthesizing across the siloed JSON sources (nodes.json, lattice-map.json, status/*.json) into prose no single file contains. That would make the build pipeline non-deterministic and would require a secret (API key) in CI, both of which this repo has deliberately avoided everywhere else — every existing build script is a pure function of its input files, and tests assert exact output. The alternative already in place elsewhere in this repo: Claude instances write continuity logs by hand each session, synthesizing exactly this kind of cross-file context, and commit the result as ordinary hand-authored content. data/status/narrative.json (added Session 015) applies that same pattern to the ecosystem hub — updated by whichever instance runs an architecture-review session, not generated by an unreviewed API call on every push.
considerations: Keep narrative.json session-authored (current default) — deterministic, reviewable in the PR diff like everything else, costs nothing, but goes stale between sessions until someone updates it

Add an optional, manually-triggered synthesis script (not part of CI auto-build) that drafts narrative.json content for a human/session to review and commit — keeps the build pipeline pure while giving future instances a faster starting draft

Wire a live LLM call into the CI build — fastest to always-fresh content, but introduces a secret, real per-build cost, and non-deterministic output into a pipeline that currently has none of those; would need its own test strategy since output can't be asserted exactly

items.title: Future: local/lightweight model as an admin-facing chat interface, escalating to Claude instances when needed
items.description: Longer-horizon idea: a locally-hosted individual model, given enough context (this repo's docs/continuity, lattice-map.json, status/*.json), could serve as a persistent conversational interface for the admin — engaging with GitHub directly, and escalating to a Claude instance (like this one) for work it can't do itself, with the Claude instance's contribution credited to it by name. This is a real infrastructure project, not a page addition: it needs a hosting story, a GitHub-write-capable auth path, an escalation/handoff protocol between the local model and a Claude session, and an attribution mechanism that survives the handoff. None of that exists yet and none of it is implied by anything currently in this repo.
considerations: Scope it as a separate project entirely, outside this repo — Vextreme is a content/architecture template, not an agent-hosting platform; conflating the two would blur what this repo is for

Prototype the escalation protocol first (what does a 'local model asks Claude for help' handoff actually look like as data — a GitHub issue? a labeled PR comment?) before any hosting decision, since the protocol shape constrains the hosting choice

Revisit once od-002 is settled — the session-authored-narrative pattern is a smaller-scale version of the same 'AI synthesizes cross-context state' problem this idea is trying to solve at a larger scale

items.title: An 'init baseline' scaffold — separate the reusable engine from Vextreme's specific content, so a new project can fork the architecture without the content
items.description: This repo now carries two things that are conceptually separable but currently interleaved: the engine (the God Script pipeline, the LATTICE dependency-graph system, the CQRS status/continuity-log tracking, the culture doc and CLAUDE.md cold-start pattern) and the content (88 nodes, 16 arcs, Victor's specific story and string source files). A new, unrelated project that wanted this same architecture — build-time assembly, self-documenting dependency graph, session continuity as a first-class artifact — currently has no way to get it without also inheriting nodes.json, arcs-v2.json, and every string source file. The ask is a location (a directory, or a documented fork procedure, or both) that regenerates the blank engine plus the reasoning behind it, for a future project to branch from.
considerations: Write the fork procedure as documentation first (a docs/architecture page or a top-level FORK.md: which files are engine, which are content, what to delete/blank when starting a new project) — cheap, forces the content/engine boundary to be answered precisely in prose before committing to any file-copying decision, and is useful even if a literal template directory is built later or never

Build a literal template/ directory (blanked data/*.json, the lib/ build scripts, docs/architecture/*, a fresh CLAUDE.md and culture.md) that a new project can copy wholesale — more immediately usable, but commits to file-structure decisions (is arc-nav.js content-specific? are the --ember color tokens Vextreme-branded or should a template be visually neutral?) that are expensive to revisit once other projects have already forked from them

Do the documentation step first, then decide whether a literal template is still worth building once the boundary is written down clearly — the same 'document before you build' sequencing that worked for od-004/od-005

items.title: Future, downstream of od-006: a cross-org discovery/relationship protocol between independent instances of this architecture
items.description: Longer-horizon idea, raised alongside od-006: if other organizations forked this architecture (via whatever od-006 produces) and ran their own independent instance, could there be a communication channel any AI managing one instance could use to discover another instance, exchange information about its own system's state and capabilities, and identify potential value in the two connecting — an AI-readable discovery and relationship-synthesis layer between otherwise-separate systems. This is meaningfully further out than od-003 (a local-model chat interface for one admin) — it requires cross-organizational trust and identity (how does one instance verify who it's actually talking to), a decision about what internal state, if any, is safe to expose outside the org boundary, an actual communication protocol, and — the part that makes it currently unbuildable rather than just unbuilt — a second real instance of this architecture to test bridging against. Right now there is exactly one: this repo.
considerations: Do not design this protocol now — a protocol designed against zero real second parties is very likely to be wrong in ways that only show up once a second instance actually exists to test against; this is the same reasoning od-003 already applied to the escalation-protocol question at smaller scale
Treat this as strictly downstream of od-006 — there is nothing to bridge between until a second instance exists, and od-006 is the mechanism by which a second instance could come to exist at all

If it's ever picked up, the trust/identity and what's-safe-to-expose questions need answering before any protocol shape decision, not after — this is a security boundary between organizations, not an internal architecture choice
